name: Graphs
coreIntuition: A graph is any relationship between things. Two cities connected by a road? Graph. A task that depends on another task? Graph. Friends on a social network? Graph. Mastering graphs means learning to **see graph structure in problems that don't mention graphs**. The nodes are the 'things', the edges are the 'relationships', and the question is always about the structure of those relationships — connectivity, shortest paths, cycles, orderings.
mentalModel: When you see a problem with entities and relationships, draw it as dots and lines. Then ask: (1) Is the graph directed or undirected? (2) Are edges weighted? (3) What am I being asked about — connectivity (BFS/DFS/Union-Find), shortest path (BFS/Dijkstra), ordering (topological sort), or something else? The graph representation (adjacency list vs matrix) is a detail — the insight is recognizing the graph and knowing what question you're answering about its structure.
whenToUse: The problem involves entities with relationships (connections, dependencies, transitions)
You're asked about connectivity, reachability, or connected components
The problem involves shortest/longest paths between states
There are dependencies between tasks (topological ordering)
A grid or matrix is given and you need to explore regions (grid = implicit graph)

thinkingFramework.detail: What are the 'things'? What are the 'relationships'? Sometimes nodes aren't obvious — in a word ladder, each word is a node and edges connect words differing by one letter.
Directed or undirected? Weighted or unweighted? Can there be cycles? Is it sparse or dense? Each answer narrows down the right algorithm.

thinkingFramework.step: 3. Identify what you're being asked about
thinkingFramework.detail: Connectivity → BFS/DFS/Union-Find. Shortest path (unweighted) → BFS. Shortest path (weighted) → Dijkstra/Bellman-Ford. Ordering with dependencies → Topological sort. This mapping is your compass.

Adjacency list for sparse graphs (most interview problems), adjacency matrix for dense graphs. For grids, you often don't need to build an explicit graph — just iterate neighbors with direction arrays.
Disconnected graphs (process ALL components), self-loops, parallel edges, empty graphs. Always ask: does every node get visited?

commonTraps.trap: Forgetting to mark nodes as visited → infinite loops
commonTraps.howToAvoid: Always maintain a visited set. For BFS, mark visited WHEN ADDING TO QUEUE, not when processing. This prevents adding the same node multiple times.

commonTraps.trap: Using DFS for shortest path in unweighted graphs
commonTraps.howToAvoid: DFS does NOT find shortest paths. BFS does. DFS finds A path; BFS finds the SHORTEST path (in unweighted graphs). This is a fundamental distinction.
If the problem asks about the whole graph, iterate over ALL nodes and start a new traversal for each unvisited node.

commonTraps.trap: Treating a grid problem as a pure array problem
commonTraps.howToAvoid: If a grid problem asks about regions, connected areas, or paths, it's a graph problem. Each cell is a node, and adjacent cells are edges.

interviewVariantStrategy: Interviewers love disguising graph problems: 'find if you can transform word A to word B' (word ladder = BFS on implicit graph), 'find the order to take courses' (topological sort), 'find connected regions in a grid' (BFS/DFS flood fill). The variant strategy: whenever you see entities + relationships + a question about reachability/ordering/shortest-path, DRAW THE GRAPH. Even if the problem never says 'graph'. Then apply the standard graph algorithms. Also watch for state-space graphs: each 'state' of a puzzle is a node, and each 'move' is an edge — this turns puzzles into shortest-path problems.

resources.title: William Fiset — Graph Theory Playlist
resources.why: The most comprehensive free graph theory course — 40+ videos from basics to advanced algorithms with animations

resources.title: CP-Algorithms — Graph Theory
resources.why: Reference-quality explanations of every graph algorithm you'll need, with correct implementations

resources.title: VisuAlgo — Graph Traversal
resources.why: Watch BFS and DFS execute step-by-step on various graph structures

resources.title: Striver — Graph Series
resources.why: Interview-focused graph problems organized by technique with detailed video solutions
